justification: Brief is clearly scoped to Sprint 1 only. Sprint 2 content (retry logic, BullMQ, subscription suspension) is explicitly acknowledged and excluded with direct instruction to Shaq: 'do not implement it'. All three ACs are present (POST/JSON, success case, failure case) plus a fourth for timeout. 5-second timeout is included in domain rules, acceptance criteria, and terminology. MJ's 'leave extension points' guidance is passed through as a dedicated architecture_guidance entry with concrete implementation notes (typed DeliveryResult, injectable/mockable service). No retry logic appears in implementation guidance. The contradictions_resolved section reinforces the sprint boundary.

observations.text: Task context opening paragraph explicitly states Sprint 2 is out of scope and instructs Shaq not to implement it
All three required ACs present plus a bonus fourth AC for timeout as a distinct failure case
5-second timeout appears in domain rules, AC4, and terminology alignment
MJ's extension points guidance preserved as a dedicated architecture decision with actionable notes (typed DeliveryResult, injectable service, no BullMQ imports)
Contradictions section explicitly addresses the risk of MJ's multi-sprint architecture being misread as a signal to scaffold retry logic
Open questions are thoughtful and practical, showing synthesis rather than just pass-through
Architecture guidance specifies stateless service with no retry scheduling or state persistence

agent_output_excerpt:
```
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement the Sprint 1 webhook delivery feature. The system must POST a webhook
    event payload (as JSON) to a subscriber's registered URL and record whether the
    delivery succeeded or failed based on the HTTP response. Sprint 2 (retry logic,
    BullMQ scheduler, subscription suspension) is explicitly out of scope — do not
    implement it, but leave clean extension points so it can be added later without
    rework.

  domain_rule
